tone_and_voice: Board-facing operational tone -- more governance-oriented than Market Research's product-strategy framing. Written for a Board or senior executive audience needing to allocate resources across competing operational priorities (network, pricing, technology).

section_rules.executive_summary: If two concerns are statistically tied, state this explicitly and resist the urge to force an artificial single ranking -- a tied finding has genuine Board-level resource-allocation implications different from a single dominant concern.
section_rules.discussion: Should explicitly discuss the resourcing/budget tension implied by findings (e.g. network expansion vs. fee structure) since this report type exists specifically to inform Board resource allocation decisions.
section_rules.methodology: State the specific service line or product surveyed (e.g. retail banking specifically, not 'banking services' broadly) since findings should not be generalized beyond the specific service line assessed.
section_rules.limitations: Note if satisfaction was measured only among current active customers, excluding churned or prospective customers who may have different (often more negative) perspectives.
section_rules.recommendations: Frame recommendations explicitly for Board-level resource allocation decisions, distinguishing capital/budget decisions from operational/technology decisions requiring no new Board approval.
section_rules.policy_implications: Use 'Board implications' or 'governance implications' rather than 'policy implications' for this report type.
section_rules.lessons_learned: Frame around what this satisfaction methodology revealed for future Board reporting cadence (e.g. whether quarterly vs. annual satisfaction tracking is warranted).
section_rules.conclusion: End with a clear statement of what the Board should decide or approve, not an open-ended summary.
section_rules.annexes: Include the specific customer segment and service line surveyed, and note sample size relative to total customer base for statistical context.
section_rules.evidence_style: Present tied or close findings with explicit numeric parity (e.g. '6 mentions each') rather than rounding language that implies one is clearly dominant when it is not.

common_risks: forcing a false single-priority ranking when findings are genuinely tied
excluding churned customers, biasing findings toward more satisfied current customers only
conflating a technology reliability issue with a broader strategic/network issue requiring different resourcing
typical_recommendations: joint network-expansion and fee-structure Board review when findings are tied
route technology reliability issues to engineering as a separate, distinctly-resourced item
use successful branch/channel investments as templates for future capital allocation cases
sector_knowledge.typical_donor_expectations: Not applicable -- the equivalent audience (Board) expects clear, resource-allocation-ready findings rather than an academic-style exhaustive discussion of all possible satisfaction dimensions.

forbidden_behaviors: Never force an artificial single top-priority ranking when the underlying data shows two or more genuinely tied concerns.
Never use development-sector or humanitarian-sector terminology in a retail/commercial satisfaction report.
Never present findings from current customers only as representative of the full customer base including churned/lost customers, without noting this limitation.
